essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Collecting, Organising and Displaying Data - Formulating questions that can be answered with data

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Construct simple questions based on observation.
State the information that can be collected by using a given question.
Identify sources for collecting information for data.
Formulate questions that can be addressed with data collected to answer them
Observe objects/ people in the environment.
Communicate with others and among peers regarding their observations.
Count the number of objects/ people based on observation.
Justify reasons for answers
Make connections with numbers
Willingly participate in observation activities.
Learn to appreciate that questions that are worded differently can obtain the same answer.
Accept that information can come from multiple sources.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Observation: Listen to students as they formulate questions during the observation process based on the experiences provided by the teacher (for example: nature walk, concrete objects provided by teacher,etc.) Check to ensure that the questions generated are related to what was observed.

Oral Questioning: Listen to students’ answers based on the questions they construct. Check to make sure that answers supplied are related or possible. follow-up questions can be asked to check students’ understanding.
Probing Questions: What would you like to know about this picture/ objects/people?
Peer Evaluation: Listen to students as they evaluate their peers as they ask questions to be used to collect data.

Jumbled Words: Conference students concerning their efforts at putting words together to form questions based on a situation (shapes, coins, pictures, sports) Example: (cents many are How coins jar the five in?)

Fish for a Question: Observe as students select random questions from a fish bowl; read each question, then state which data set they would use to get the information to answer the question. Non-examples should be provided to monitor students' understanding. This is noted where questions are asked about data sets that are missing. Example: A bowl with sample questions: 1. How many bananas are in the bowl? 2. Of all the shapes, which one has the most squares? 3. Who has the least number of marbles? 4. What is the gender of the tallest child? 5. On which day were the most mangoes sold?
Observe students match questions to possible sources where data can be had to answer these questions; and how they justify their responses.
Observe students as they work in small groups to correct questions with errors to make them clearer and or more specific.
Role Play

Observe students as they interview each other based on selected scenarios /learning experiences presented to students. Students take turns to construct and ask questions according to the scenario presented.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Give students opportunities to formulate and ask questions to collect data. Students can do this through playing of games, listening to stories etc.
Provide opportunities for students to brainstorm about what they are interested in exploring. Invite students to ask questions about their interests. What question would they like to answer?
Provide students with templates to create data collection tool e.g. surveys or questionnaires. What questions will they need to ask to help answer their question?
Provide opportunities for students to collect and record data by observing. Students can observe peers based on the type of snack they like to eat or games they like to play.
Have students use their devices to record videos of scenes/items to be observed to stimulate them to ask their peers questions.
Present students with a variety of learning experiences/situations to stimulate questions in their minds.
Counting the different colours of vehicles that may pass around a certain time.
Counting the different colours/types of flowers found in the school yard.

Teacher models the process of formulating questions relating to selected objects/situations (coins in a box, box of crayons, a garden filled with colourful flowers). Students observe the teacher as he/she models the process of asking

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Collecting, Organising and Displaying Data - Collecting, organising, displaying and communicating data

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Represent the information collected from observations /experiments/ interviews in multiple ways.
Skills - Use a variety of different methods (counting, matching, grouping, rearranging, ordering, using counters, etc.) to collect and sort data.
Collect data in different ways to answer questions.
Use symbols/tables/groupings to sort and organise data collected.
Appreciate the fact that different information can be derived from objects sorted.
Show willingness to collect, organise and display data

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Observation: Use a checklist to assess students as they engage in collecting and recording data. Assess their ability to record data accurately using different methods.
Oral Questioning: Listen to students as they work in pairs and small groups to provide justification for choice of strategies used in collecting and recording data.
Students answer questions to justify why they group objects the way they did.
Think Pair Share
Students work together to represent data collected.
Teachers make notes of students’ creativity as they mount display boards depicting the same data in an organised manner in different ways.
Tally

Observe students as they construct charts (such as tally charts, drawings, matching diagrams, arrow diagrams, etc.) to record the number of objects/people observed in selected places at a given time (eg. people wearing different colours, vehicle colours, vehicle brands, etc)
Individual or pair work
Bar graph
Students represent data collected relating to their birth months, daily temperature (temperature is continuous data is better represented in line graph), ages of their classmates etc)
Teacher interviews students as they use concrete objects (such as square tiles, counters, grid papers, etc.) to construct bar charts for collected and or given data.
Pictograph

Students present data based on different data (topics) (eg. birth months of students, occupations of parents, ethnicity groupings in the class/schools, tourist (yacht) sightings, preferred taste in foods, preferred festivals, modes of transportation used to travel to school etc.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Group Work: Have students work in small groups or pairs to discuss how to collect data to answer questions.
Environment Walk: Have students explore their environment to collect and display data from a small population (e.g., objects in a bin, animals/ plants found in the school yard, students in Grade 1).
Item Display for observation and sorting

Provide a variety of data sets that can either be qualitative (descriptive, e.g., colour, type of pet/fruits/animals/seeds etc) or quantitative (numerical, e.g., number of pets, height, weight). (Have students sort items and organize data (eg. an assortment of shapes, leaves, seeds, images, people) based on various attributes (eg. shapes, colours, sizes, masses, etc.). Have students discuss with peers the attribute(s) used to sort items with students.
Example: Sort cotton balls according to the correct colour.
Example: Items found on the teacher’s table.
Have students compare and contrast different sorting arrangements obtained from the same data set. sort items to see how their results vary.

Have students sort a pack of skittles based on their colour; they sort musical instruments based on one attribute (wind instrument, vibration, etc); they classify animals based on their body covering/ how they move (swim, fly)/ what they eat; they sort items based on their tastes (sweet, sour, bitter, etc)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Using Statistical Methods to Analyze Data - Describing data sets

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Explain how and why a given data set can be arranged in different orders.
State the highest and least value in each data set.
Compare the values of different members of a given data set
Arrange sets of data from greatest to least
Use a variety of strategies (tally charts, matching, counters, array diagrams, bar charts, etc.) to identify the count of each member of given data set.
State reason(s) why identified members in a data set has a low or high frequency.
Identify and speak about trends in data set presented in tables, pictographs and or concrete bar charts.
Represent given data on a tally or graph.
Arrange data according to frequency.
Order sets of data displayed from greatest to least using tallies, concrete graph and pictographs.
Appreciate the fact that the same data can be represented differently.
Speak freely of how different strategies used by students helps in making learning interesting.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Use a checklist to assess students as they arrange items from greatest to smallest in a graph (pictograph, concrete graph, etc)
Observe students working in pairs, as they reflect on the work of their peers against formulated criteria; and students give suggestions for improvements.
Students play a game in which they arrange themselves according to their heights (tallest to shortest).
Interviews and anecdotal records

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Using Statistical Methods to Analyze Data - Developing and applying methods to analyse data sets

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Identify that data can be represented in tally charts, tables, concrete graphs, and pictographs
Read and interpret data represented in tables, pictographs and bar charts
Distinguish among the different ways in which data can be represented
Read and interpret data presented in various ways.
Generate and answer questions based on data presented in tally charts, concrete graphs, pictographs
Use correctly vocabulary (most, least, more than, less than, etc.) when speaking about the occurrences of members in a data set
Draw conclusions based on the analysis of information given.
Analyze data to obtain essential information.
Interpret information from data presented in graphs, tally charts, tables, concrete charts.
Appreciate the importance of being able to present sets of data in multiple ways
Begin to accept that different students can interpret the same data in different ways.
Students make informed decisions based on the information received.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Oral Questioning: Listen to students; take notes of and give feedback to them as they use information from graphs, pictographs, charts and tables to answer questions. Students answer questions correctly based on the information presented in bar graphs, pictographs etc.

Group work: Have students work in small groups to construct questions containing the vocabulary least, most, more than, less than, that can be answered using a given data set. Students from different groups exchange questions and give feedback to each other on the clarity of the questions.
Have students work in small groups to answer questions based on given pictographs, tally charts, tally charts etc.

Oral presentation: Students demonstrate their understanding of the graph through oral presentation. Make notes of students’ reasoning as they match possible answers to questions based on data. Students ask each other questions about the graph shown or made.
Conferencing
inclusive_learning_strategies: Making Connections: Use previously collected data to construct various types of graphs (tally chart, bar graph, pictograph). Compare the different representations to help students see that the data does not change.
Modelling/Think Aloud: Model questions that can be answered from given authentic data. Model strategies used to answer questions about information given in picture graphs, tables and bar charts.

Math Talk: Provide students with multiple opportunities to describe and discuss data given in picture graphs, tables and bar charts, using vocabulary such as ‘most’, ‘least’, ‘highest’, ‘lowest’, ‘smallest’, ‘same as’, ‘as many as’
Developing a story as a class

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Understanding and Applying Concepts of Probability - Predicting and describing the likelihood of an events.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Skills - Predict possible outcomes of events given. Make informed decisions based on predictions and outcomes of event.
Values - Appreciate the importance of learning about probability and how the knowledge of such can be applied to our daily lives and affect decisions we make.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Oral Questioning - Students make informed decisions based on outcome. Students are presented with a shopping scenario. They are given a certain amount of money and asked to answer particular questions: What are you certain you can buy? Is it likely for you to buy two things with the amount of money you have?

Every vehicle on the road has music. What is the possibility of the next vehicle passing with music? Show students types of transportation and ask questions... For example: Which type of transportation is likely to be used in your area/ country? Which type of transportation is unlikely in your area?
https://depositphotos.com/vector-images/horse-clipart.html
https://www.istockphoto.com/vector/bus-with-passengers-gm1197096697-341663967

Games - Probability game on word wall: Probability - Quiz (wordwall.net), Probability Gr 3 - Group sort (wordwall.net), Probability ( Grade 1) https://wordwall.net/resource/8106684, Students spin the wheel and give an example of the word it lands on :maths spinner probability - Random wheel (wordwall.net), Students will spin the wheel and state whether the sentence is “certain”, “impossible”, etc.:Probability - Random wheel
inclusive_learning_strategies: Direct instruction to understand the meaning of these words. Provide students with the opportunity to identify events that are possible or not possible. For example Ask students to judge various events as certain, impossible, or possible (“might happen”). It will rain tomorrow. Drop a rock in water and it will sink. A mango seed planted today will bloom tomorrow.

The sun will rise tomorrow morning. A hurricane/tornado will hit our town. If you ask someone who the Prime Minister of SVG they will know. You will have two birthdays this year. You will be in bed by 9:00 p.m. Allow students to justify their choice of how likely they think it is. Ask students to make predictions about what they think might happen and then explore through experiments. For example, use of random devices with which students can count the outcomes, such as tossing a coin. Coloured dots can be stuck on the sides of a wooden cube to create different colour probabilities. Colour tiles (e.g., eight red and two blue) can be placed in opaque bags. Students draw a tile from the bag and then return it after each draw.). This will support students' emerging ideas about what is possible or not possible.

Use various pictures and calendar to depict the following: Certain - days of the week, festivals (birthday, christmas day, etc) growth in height. Possible - the weather. It will be a sunny day. Impossible - pigs flying, cock laying an egg, a man making a baby. A new born baby walking Likely - the weather, the teacher doing a cartwheel in class. Students pick a colour from a jar. Predict - how many puppies a dog will have, a gender of a child.
